Celebrate the milestone of public German discourse
The lobby after the event. People milling about, coffee in paper cups. Verumius and Yilmaz find each other near the exit.
Simply: 'Important contribution.' Two words. She nods. Maximum meaning.
Thank you. I chose every word carefully.
I heard that. And the panel did too.
They drink their coffee in comfortable silence. Through the window, Berlin glows. Verumius has spoken German at a public debate. Not survived it. Contributed to it.
On the U-Bahn home, his phone buzzes. A letter from the tax office. 47 words, one sentence. The glow fades. Reality returns.
I am of the view that
The formal position statement. Stronger than 'Ich denke.'\nAcademic and legal standard.

A: What is your opinion?
B: I am of the view that we must act.
A: And why?
B: Because time is pressing.
'I am of the view that.' The formal position statement. Stronger than 'Ich denke' or 'Ich glaube.' You have an Auffassung (understanding/view), not just an opinion. Academic and legal standard.
in my view
Ansicht from 'ansehen' (to look at). Dative case: meiner.\nThe polite but firm positioning phrase.

A: In my view, we need reform.
B: I see it that way too.
A: In my view, the compromise is fair.
B: We can build on that.
'In my view.' 'Ansicht' from 'ansehen' (to look at). Your view of the matter. 'Meiner Ansicht nach brauchen wir Reform.' Dative case: meiner. The polite but firm positioning phrase.
it is beyond question that
The phrase that closes debate on a point.\nEstablishes something as uncontested fact.

A: Is that certain?
B: It is beyond question that fundamental rights apply.
A: It is beyond question that we must act.
B: On that we agree.
'It is beyond question that.' The phrase that closes debate on a point. 'Es steht ausser Frage, dass die Grundrechte gelten.' When you want to establish something as uncontested fact.
to counter that, it must be said that
Dem (dative) + entgegenzuhalten (to hold against).\nElegant, indirect, devastating. The formal objection.

A: The argument seems strong.
B: To counter that, the data is outdated.
A: A good objection.
B: Facts must be current.
'To counter that, it must be said that.' Dem (dative) + entgegenzuhalten (to hold against). Elegant, indirect, devastating. You do not attack. You present what must be held against the argument.
it cannot be denied that
Leugnen (to deny). The concession marker that acknowledges\ninconvenient truth. Honest, then move on.

A: Are there problems?
B: It cannot be denied that there are some.
A: But there is also progress.
B: That cannot be denied either.
'It cannot be denied that.' 'Leugnen' (to deny). The concession marker that acknowledges inconvenient truth. Honest, then move on.
from that follows
'Sich ergeben' (to result). The logical consequence phrase.\nThe evidence produces the conclusion organically.

A: The majority has decided.
B: From that follows the necessity to act.
A: From that also follows responsibility.
B: For all involved.
'From that follows.' 'Sich ergeben' (to result). The logical consequence phrase. 'Daraus ergibt sich die Notwendigkeit zu handeln.' The evidence produces the conclusion organically.
in a nutshell, to sum up
Brought to the point. The summary marker.\nEverything condensed to its essence.

A: How would you summarize it?
B: In a nutshell: we need dialogue.
A: To sum up: more willingness to compromise.
B: Exactly.
'Brought to the point.' The summary marker. 'Auf den Punkt gebracht: Wir brauchen Dialog.' Everything condensed to its essence. The German 'in a nutshell.'
all in all
The comprehensive summary. Everything considered,\neverything weighed. The final assessment.

A: How was the debate?
B: All in all, very productive.
A: All in all a success?
B: Yes, despite some disagreements.
'All in all.' 'Alles in allem war es eine produktive Debatte.' The comprehensive summary. Everything considered, everything weighed. The final assessment.
in the face of, in view of
An (at) + Gesichts (face). Takes genitive.\nYou look the situation in the face.

A: In the face of the challenges...
B: ...we must work together.
A: In view of the situation?
B: Quick action is necessary.
An (at) + Gesichts (face). In the face of, in view of. Takes genitive: 'Angesichts der Herausforderungen.' You look the situation in the face. Formal written register.
on the basis of, due to
Auf (on) + Grund (ground/reason). Takes genitive.\nStanding on the ground of evidence.

A: Why was it rejected?
B: Due to the concerns.
A: On the basis of the results...
B: ...we must change the strategy.
Auf (on) + Grund (ground/reason). On the basis of, due to. Takes genitive: 'Aufgrund der Ergebnisse.' Standing on the ground of evidence. Can be written 'auf Grund' (two words).
regardless of, irrespective of
Un- + geachtet (regarded). Takes genitive.\nYou have regarded and you disregard anyway.

A: Is there resistance?
B: Regardless of the criticism, it continues.
A: Regardless of the costs?
B: Yes, the cause is more important.
Un- + geachtet (regarded). Regardless of, irrespective of. Takes genitive: 'Ungeachtet der Bedenken.' You have regarded the concerns, and you disregard them anyway. Formal, decisive.
in favor of
Zu (to) + Gunsten (favor). Takes genitive.\nOn whose side the decision falls.

A: Who benefits?
B: In favor of the minority.
A: The decision was made in favor of the plaintiffs.
B: An important signal.
Zu (to) + Gunsten (favor). In favor of. Takes genitive: 'Zugunsten der Minderheit.' The preposition of benefit. On whose side the decision falls.
to polarize
From Greek 'polos' (axis/pole). Pulling people to\nopposite poles. Seen as dangerous in German politics.

A: The debate polarizes.
B: Yes, there is hardly any center left.
A: Polarizing harms democracy.
B: That is why we need more dialogue.
From Greek 'polos' (axis/pole). To polarize. 'Die Debatte polarisiert.' Pulling people to opposite poles. In German politics, Polarisierung is seen as dangerous to the democratic center.
to put into perspective, to relativize
From Latin 'relativus.' Making something relative,\nreducing its absolute weight. Can be positive or negative.

A: Should one put the result into perspective?
B: No, the numbers speak for themselves.
A: Critics relativize the successes.
B: That is unfair.

From Latin 'relativus.' To put into perspective, to relativize. 'Man sollte das Ergebnis nicht relativieren.' Making something relative, reducing its absolute weight. Can be positive (nuance) or negative (downplaying).
to reject, to dismiss
Zurück (back) + weisen (to direct). Separable.\nStronger than ablehnen. You direct it back.

A: The accusation was rejected.
B: Rightly so?
A: Yes, it was unfounded.
B: Then the rejection was correct.
Zurück (back) + weisen (to direct). To reject, to dismiss. 'Den Vorwurf zurückweisen.' Separable. You direct it back where it came from. Stronger than ablehnen.
to call into question, to challenge
In + Frage (question) + stellen (to place). Not rejecting,\nbut questioning the validity.

A: Can one call the decision into question?
B: Yes, everyone has the right to.
A: He challenges the method.
B: A legitimate criticism.

In + Frage (question) + stellen (to place). To call into question, to challenge. 'Die Entscheidung infrage stellen.' You place something inside a question mark. Not rejecting, but questioning its validity.
to agree with, to concur
Bei (by/with) + pflichten (duty/to agree). Formal agreement.\nOld-fashioned but still used in formal debate.

A: Do you agree?
B: I fully and completely concur.
A: May I agree with you?
B: Of course.

Bei (by/with) + pflichten (duty/to agree). To agree with, to concur. 'Ich pflichte Ihnen bei.' Formal agreement. You place your obligation alongside theirs. Old-fashioned but still used in formal debate.
to defuse, to de-escalate
Ent- (un-) + schaerfen (to sharpen). Literally: to un-sharpen.\nRemoving the edge from a conflict.

A: The situation is tense.
B: We must defuse it.
A: How can one de-escalate the conflict?
B: Through dialogue and concessions.
Ent- (un-) + schaerfen (to sharpen). To defuse, to de-escalate. 'Die Situation entschaerfen.' Literally: to un-sharpen. Removing the edge from a conflict.
